07 Growth + Marketing AMA with Amara Omoregie  04 26 22 - YouTube.mp4
For those of you that don't know me, I'm in the industry. I've been in sales since I was 19. 2003, 2004. I don't know what the math is on that. It's been a while. Um... So I love sales. I also have been marketing about the same amount of time, give or take a year or two. I've worked with Fortune 10 companies, Fortune 100, Fortune 1000, Fortune 5000. But I really love small businesses. I think that's where we can make the most impact as marketers. They already have a ton of money. Who cares about them? The big ones. I'm just kidding. There's just a lot of red tape. So I'm here to bestow any knowledge that I can upon you guys. So this is your chance to ask any questions.
I know we're now diving into growth engines over the last week or so. And so that's always fun. So please leave your questions in the comments or in the chat. And Carly will facilitate those because I can't sometimes miss the chat when I'm on a roll. Um. Yeah, we can talk growth engines. We can talk. marketing challenges, hiring. funnel issues, sales, whatever. I'm here for you guys. It's a full hour for you guys to get your questions answered. For those of you that are just sitting back and just want to listen to people. It's fun and all, but I feel like you have a lot more fun if you have a question. And don't think it's a dumb question because I promise you, your question is also the same as somebody else's question.
So just go ahead and ask it. It's totally okay. So. Yeah, thanks, Amara. Yeah, you guys. Can feel free to come off mute, get the ball rolling. Any and all questions, you know, nothing's off limits here. I know it's kind of hard to be the first person to step up. But yeah, like Amara said, we have a full hour together. Definitely want to take advantage of her expertise. Awesome. Henvig, it looks like you have your hand raised. You can come off mute if you'd like. question about Facebook. Are you... It's kind of, you gotta kind of, there's a lot going on with Facebook as far as, especially with best practices. I only um I've run one round of the program, but I haven't done like any advertising I've just done. One-to-one outreach so far, so now I'm trying to kind of go a little bit out of my contact them. Direct relationships to reach more people. Okay, got it. Um, okay so you're just starting. There's a couple things that you'll want to um so. There's Facebook ads and there's Google ads. There's a lot of different ads platforms for that matter. One thing that you'll want to understand when we attack. When you try to utilize Facebook ads. A lot of people like Facebook ads because it's a little bit easier. To understand it's a little bit easier to set up than Google. Google's got a lot of buttons. And so. One thing I would say is.
Um, be strategic. So one of the things about Facebook is that there's lower intent. So on Google, people are searching for keywords. Um, that closely aligns with your product or service. So let's say you're, what's your product about? Your online product about? It's for language learning. Okay. So someone could literally Google, 'I want to learn a second language' and you can show up for that keyword and have a ton of relevancy and intent. Because if someone's searching for that keyword, they're not willy-nilly looking for that. On Facebook, unfortunately, that's not the case. So there's different types of audiences that you can advertise to on Facebook. If you're already getting some traffic, are you getting any kind of traffic on your site at all? Yeah. Yeah, I would say so, just through quite organic content.
Facebook is fantastic for retargeting and remarketing. So I would start there. You'll get the best bang for your buck. Um. Yeah. Okay. So that would, I think. Technically speaking, that would mean doing probably a little bit more in terms of because I was wondering how much do I need to kind of sync up my website with. Um my Facebook. You'll need to create, you'll need to add your pixel to your website. Um and then there's also conversion API as well. That you'll need to sync up. And it has step-by-step instructions. There for how to do it, but it's not very straightforward. You kind of got to know what you're doing with it. So you might want to just hire someone to help you get set up. Just so you don't.
I don't want to say you'll mess it up, but just so that like. It's set up properly because your biggest bang for your buck is going to be retargeting. Targeting cold audiences is going to be a little bit. challenging. And so in our old growth engine training. For SOS, we had swim lanes, and so... Well, basically it just reflected the customer journey. So you have the awareness stage, your engagement stage, your subscribe stage, your convert stage. What you want to do is, once someone comes to your site, So if you're retargeting organic traffic, meaning they've already come to your site, they're not cold— or they know it's not that they've never heard of you. It's a lot more warm. They have a better chance of converting if they come back again.
So your goal is to get them to come back again and do that next logical thing. So, if you have an offer like an ebook or some sort of entry-level offer to get them back to the site— you want to. How to do a retargeting campaign. For anyone who's visited the site in the last [i] don't know how many days and then get them to convert in that way. So it does require a little bit of strategy because you need to kind of understand how to segment your audience using your website and using their behaviors and their interests. And create ads. And target those ads to those audiences based on their next logical step. Let's say someone already downloaded an e-book. You want to exclude those people from this retargeting list.
And now do more bottom of funnel tactics, which is like sending them testimonials. Doing more hard-hitting sales stuff. Um, cause they've already converted, you know, information wise, and then you can follow up emails as well. So it's kind of a. It's not super like. It's not just doing the Facebook ads. There's some strategy involved, so. You might hire someone to at least just consult with you on how to like set it up and architect it. architect yeah thank you i have actually i reached out to somebody today so about just that getting set up So I'm glad I'm on the sort of on the right track. Um, seven dollar is a video course about playing guitar solos, but about only one part of of that skill and the upsell is more complex, complete course about the topic. And we are doing that. But the upsell is not working at all. We've got zero upsell purchase. So my question is more general because the offer is not in English, so I can show you, but it won't tell you anything, but some general guidelines, how we can go about optimizing it.
Um... So... $7 or $2. 97. So $7 is a very interesting thing. I would test the off. I would test your front end offer at like 30 or something like that. Because when you do that big of a jump, it's very, um, because when you do a $7 offer, you're playing in the free world. Like. You're in a really unfortunate industry where people can get a lot of information on the website for free. And so $3 to $7 isn't that big of a jump. So you're probably getting a very low quality hobbyist type group of people that are just like, 'Ah, I just got a new guitar for my birthday. Want to play some cool stuff?
You guys just kind of scratched that itch for $7.' I don't mind spending $7. Now, Who is the type of person that would want just. Who is it? Who's that person that would want to spend more than $7? And what is it that they would be wanting to try to learn? 'Mm-hmm.' is the question. And so, you know, for $297. I think That might be a different avatar. And you're going to have to write copy and write an offer that speaks to them, because. What happens in marketing, and I'm seeing this more and more, I was just talking to one of my colleagues yesterday about this is. Marketers are all about getting cheap leads. But then it— muddies everything— because now you have a group that won't upsell, or you won't, or they're unqualified.
And so you have two options. You can double the price of your offer, and see if that helps. Get you to the 297. Um. But again, you're still playing around in that free group that really doesn't have anything to lose by spending $14 instead of seven. On a book, but I would start there, test the offer at a little bit of a higher end to see if that. Gets you a better quality lead. And also do some research on who wants to spend this money, who wants to spend. More on... lessons and why would they want to spend more? Because if it's just to get access to new songs, that might not be enough. If it's... I'm going to pay $297 and I'm going to be able to get on a stage and play in a band.
That's a different avatar with a different reason why they want to. Play guitar. Because again, people play guitar for different reasons. He's got to find that one reason he was willing to pay for. Mm-hmm. Do you think it's a good idea to just survey the previous customers of that core offer? This upsell offer, because from that growth engine we have none, but from other, from our email list we've got many customers. So maybe I could just survey them and find their I think that's perfect. Okay when when when they um, when they buy, yes? You mean? Okay, okay. Okay, so to conclude, you said to raise the price to make it more... Test it at double and see what happens. So about $30, $40. It's not in dollars. You can run a few tests. Run one to a landing page where it's $14 and then run one at like... 21 between 21 and 30. And see what happens. I don't want it to screw up your whole funnel. I would run. Three different tests to see. Which like how the conversion rate functions.
Cause you'd be surprised. Maybe you're sometimes raising the price, increases your conversion rate. Cause perceived value is a whole other. Part of the conversation too. Okay. Okay. You're going to see some messiness when it comes to open rates. Just across the board. Yeah, so we have looked at the iOS updates. It seems to be more significant than just that. I'm not— I'm not extremely familiar with the, like, the technical aspects of it, but what you're saying is it would drop the open rate, but you're like, click through would be the same, basically. Depends. I mean, it's relative. So if you have something worth, if you have something to click, then it's, it's. Hard to say because if you don't, sometimes we put stuff to click an email, sometimes we don't. So you can't, so it's hard to say like. Your click-through rate should be X, Y, and Z, right? It kind of depends on the purpose of the email.
We UTM tag ours so we can measure. A little bit better. So that we're not just making generalizations about what our click-through rate should be. Our bottom of funnel click-through rate's a lot higher. Then our top of funnel click-through rates sometimes. Um and just basically depending on the general purpose of the emails we use HubSpot. So we use campaigns to kind of determine what our open rates, to look at trends and open rates, click-through rates, et cetera, et cetera, on those things are. Uh, as far as I know, have you looked at your deliverability to see if your deliverability has been impacted at all? Yeah, we did. And it doesn't necessarily seem to be a deliverability issue. It started happening a couple months ago. I know Google made an update around that time as well.
And a good portion. Of our list is Gmail users, so I've been trying to look into that and see what it is. But it's like a third of our open rates— it's gone down like a third of our open rate, and everything else is stayed very similar once they're in email and the type of email is like the same thing. It's basically I know we talked a few weeks ago about how like our emails are just transactional, like they're coupon emails, is what does the best for us or like product updates. And so it's the same like image to text ratio and all those sorts of things. Like, and it's offering the same percentage amount coupon with the same button in the same part of the email.
Have you tested whether or not the email is going to the promotions tab? Have you like, do you have an email account that you can test on and see if it's going to promotion staff? Um, no, I haven't, I haven't checked that specifically. Test that. So if you have a non-company, non-workplace Gmail account, like an actual like blah, blah, blah, Gmail .com. See if your emails are going to see if tested on test that and see if their emails are going straight to the promotions tab. It could be. And how would you suggest fixing that if that is the case? There's a lot of different ways to fix that.
Try putting no images in the email. Try putting no offers in there. Try...
It's a very complex question. There's a lot of different ways to. There's a lot of different ways to fix that. I would try not doing coupon codes and stuff like that because that could be triggering the promotions tab. Just see what happens, put some helpful info. Maybe tell people about. I don't know, some new trends or talk about. What's happening in technology, something educational, and see if that gets it out of the promotions tab. Start there. I would start there. First run the test, see if that's what's happening, because if it's going down that much and you're running coupons and stuff. It's because of the promotions tab, I would have to guess. You have to switch up your content. Like, put a lot more content in it and then add a coupon closer to the end.
As a quick reminder or something, or maybe not call it a coupon. Call it something else. Possibly. Okay. Yeah. We don't call it a coupon necessarily. Like it'll say like bonus or something like that. Yeah. I would say like. Um, There's different language I would maybe omit and just say different. Just like, hey, now's the time. At a minimum and try to get it out of the Promotions tab. But yeah, run that test first and see what happens. I bet that's what it is. Okay. It's if your site's not, so you think about Google, it's not humans pushing it. It's algorithms, which are just like mathematical equations. And all kinds of. Trickery to like try to make sure that Google's goal is to give people what they're looking for when they're looking for it and get it right the first time. That's how they became the biggest and baddest search engine because they did the best job at it. You don't have to Google 15 different things to find what you're looking for. You can usually get it done the first or second time.
So if there's not enough info for Google to work with on your website, which in your case there isn't, they're not going to rank you for it because they don't want to make people guess. And so, and if your information on your site isn't helpful enough or doesn't give people enough info. or makes people go to your site and then leave, that's going to impact your ability to be searched and found and all that stuff.
